selection_rationale: Default non-HAL fallback per the design's open question. Included to confirm that the selection gates are not HAL-specific and to surface the broader methodology gap: OpenAI's launch paper for BrowseComp publishes single-point aggregates with no run-level data, no confidence intervals, and no cost figures. This is exactly the kind of benchmark report the rigor project is meant to challenge — but reanalysis would require data that is not public.
transformations: extracted aggregate accuracies from OpenAI's BrowseComp launch paper and confirming secondary references
encoded each evaluated configuration as one row (agent_id, model_id, browsing, accuracy, accuracy_lower, accuracy_upper, accuracy_source, note)
noted that o1's accuracy was reported only as '<10%' so it is encoded as accuracy=null with accuracy_upper=0.10
noted that GPT-4o (no browsing) was reported as 'near-zero'; encoded as accuracy=0.0 with accuracy_upper=0.01 — these are NOT confidence intervals
redaction_policy: No redaction needed: the inventory contains only paper-level aggregate accuracies that are already public. No proprietary or model-trace content.

scouting_lessons.aggregate_only_public_release: BrowseComp is a clear example of the public-release pattern this project critiques: single-point accuracies, no uncertainty, no per-task data, no cost. Even the headline comparison ('Deep Research is 85x better than GPT-4o') is a ratio of point estimates. Including this as a fallback candidate documents that the v0 toolkit cannot help until the underlying data are released — the methodology layer is downstream of transparency.
